name: Filipino Tapsilog

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Marinate the beef with the garlic for 15-20 minutes.
Pan-fry until well browned and cooked through, 3-4 minutes per side.
Serve with the rice and a fried egg.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Marinate the thinly sliced beef with the garlic, soy sauce, vinegar, and lime juice (standing in for calamansi) for at least 20-30 minutes.
Pan-fry over medium-high heat, 3-5 minutes per side, until well browned and cooked through.
Serve with garlic fried rice and a fried egg - tapsilog (a portmanteau of tapa, sinangag, and itlog) is a beloved Filipino breakfast combo.

name: Filipino Garlic Fried Rice (Sinangag)
steps: Fry the garlic in the oil until golden and fragrant.
Add the rice and stir-fry 4-5 minutes until heated through and slightly crisp.
steps_detailed: Fry the garlic in the oil over medium heat, 2-3 minutes, until golden and fragrant - watch closely, as it burns quickly.
Add the rice and black pepper, and stir-fry 5-6 minutes, breaking up clumps, until heated through and slightly crisp.
The garlic component of nearly every Filipino silog breakfast combo.

name: Filipino Longganisa with Eggs
steps: Fry the sausage with the garlic until browned.
Fry the egg separately.
Serve together with rice.
steps_detailed: Fry the sausage with the garlic and sugar over medium heat, 5-6 minutes, until browned and slightly caramelized, mimicking the sweet-garlicky character of real longganisa.
Fry the egg separately to your liking.
Serve together with garlic rice - another beloved Filipino silog breakfast combo.

name: Filipino Adobo
steps: Combine the chicken, soy sauce, vinegar, and garlic in a pot, and bring to a simmer.
Cover and cook 25-30 minutes until the chicken is tender.
Uncover and simmer 5-10 more minutes to reduce the sauce, then serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Combine the chicken, soy sauce, vinegar, garlic, bay leaf, and black pepper in a pot - importantly, don't stir once the vinegar goes in until it's had a chance to simmer uncovered briefly, which mellows its sharpness.
Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook 25-30 minutes until the chicken is tender.
Uncover and simmer 8-12 more minutes to reduce the sauce until glossy and slightly thickened.
Remove the bay leaf and serve with rice - adobo is widely considered the Philippines' national dish, with countless regional and family variations.

name: Filipino Sinigang
steps: Simmer the pork with the onion in water for 30-35 minutes until nearly tender.
Add the tomatoes and strained tamarind pulp, and simmer 10-15 more minutes.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Simmer the pork with the onion in 3 cups water for 30-35 minutes until nearly tender.
Add the tomatoes, strained tamarind pulp, and fish sauce, and simmer 10 minutes.
Add the green beans and simmer 8-10 more minutes until tender.
Serve with rice - sinigang's distinctive sour broth, most often built on tamarind, makes it one of the most beloved soups in Filipino cooking.

name: Filipino Pancit
steps: Brown the chicken, then add the cabbage and cook until just tender.
Add the cooked noodles and soy sauce, and toss everything together.
Serve hot.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken with the garlic over medium-high heat, 6-8 minutes.
Add the cabbage and carrot, and cook 4-5 minutes until just tender.
Add the cooked noodles and soy sauce, and toss everything together over high heat 3-4 minutes.
Finish with a squeeze of lime and serve hot - pancit, in its countless regional forms, is a fixture of Filipino celebrations, traditionally eaten for long life.

name: Filipino Caldereta

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Brown the beef with the onion.
Add the tomatoes, carrot, and a splash of water, and simmer, covered, 1.5-2 hours until the beef is tender.
Serve with rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Brown the beef with the onion and garlic over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the diced tomatoes, tomato paste, carrot, and beef broth.
Cover and simmer over low heat 1.5-2.5 hours until the beef is completely tender and the sauce has thickened.
Serve with rice - caldereta reflects the Philippines' Spanish colonial influence, a rich tomato-based beef stew for special occasions.

name: Filipino Sisig
steps: Brown the pork well, chopping it finer as it cooks.
Toss with the onion, lime juice, and jalapeno.
Serve sizzling hot with rice.
steps_detailed: Brown the finely chopped pork over medium-high heat, 10-12 minutes, chopping further with the spatula as it cooks until finely textured.
Toss with the onion, jalapeno, lime juice, and soy sauce.
Top with a fried egg and serve sizzling hot with rice - sisig, from Pampanga, is a beloved Filipino dish, traditionally served on a hot plate.

name: Filipino Kare-Kare

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw
Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Simmer the beef until tender, about 1.5 hours.
Blend the ground peanuts with a little broth into a paste and stir in, along with the achiote paste.
Add the green beans and simmer 10-12 minutes until tender.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw
Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Simmer the beef with the onion in water to cover for 1.5-2 hours until very tender, reserving 1/2 cup of the cooking liquid.
Blend the ground peanuts with the beef broth into a smooth paste, then stir into the pot along with the achiote paste (for its characteristic orange color).
Add the green beans and simmer 10-15 minutes until tender and the sauce has thickened.
Serve with rice and a side of fermented shrimp paste if you have it - kare-kare is a beloved Filipino oxtail-and-peanut stew, traditionally reserved for special occasions.

name: Filipino Grilled Milkfish (Bangus)
steps: Marinate the fish with the garlic, lime juice, and soy sauce for 15-20 minutes.
Grill over medium-high heat, 5-6 minutes per side, until cooked through.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Marinate the fish with the garlic, lime juice, soy sauce, and black pepper for 20-30 minutes.
Brush with the oil and grill over medium-high heat, 5-7 minutes per side, until cooked through and lightly charred.
Serve with rice - bangus, the Philippines' national fish, is prepared in countless ways, but simply grilled is among the most beloved.

name: Filipino Lechon Kawali (Crispy Pork Belly)
steps: Simmer the pork with the bay leaf and garlic in water to cover for 45-50 minutes until tender.
Drain well, pat dry, and fry in the oil until deeply golden and crackling-crisp on the outside, 4-5 minutes per side.
Slice and serve with a vinegar dipping sauce.
steps_detailed: Simmer the pork with the bay leaf, garlic, and black pepper in water to cover for 45-55 minutes until fully tender.
Drain thoroughly and pat completely dry - the drier the surface, the crispier the result.
Fry in the oil over medium-high heat, 5-7 minutes per side, until deeply golden and shatteringly crisp on the outside.
Slice and serve with a vinegar dipping sauce - the double-cooking method (boil then fry) is what gives lechon kawali its signature contrast between tender meat and crackling skin.

name: Filipino Lumpia
steps: Brown the chicken and mix with the cabbage and carrot.
Roll tightly in the wrapper, sealing with a little water.
Fry until golden and crisp, about 5-6 minutes.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground chicken with the garlic and soy sauce, then mix with the shredded cabbage and carrot, cooking briefly until just softened.
Let cool slightly, then roll tightly in the wrapper, sealing the edge with a little water.
Fry in the oil over medium heat, turning, 5-7 minutes until deeply golden and crisp on all sides - lumpia are a beloved Filipino party staple.

name: Filipino Turon
steps: Roll the banana in the wrapper with the brown sugar, sealing the edge.
Fry until golden and caramelized, about 3-4 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Roll the banana (with a few slices of jackfruit if using, a traditional addition) in the wrapper along with the brown sugar, sealing the edge tightly with a little water.
Fry in the oil over medium heat, 3-5 minutes per side, until the wrapper is deeply golden and the sugar has caramelized into a sticky glaze.
A beloved Filipino sweet snack, sold at street stalls everywhere.

name: Filipino Fish Balls with Sweet Sauce
steps: Mix the ground fish with the flour and shape into small balls.
Fry until golden, about 4-5 minutes.
Serve with a simple sweet sauce made from the sugar and a splash of water and soy sauce.
steps_detailed: Mix the finely ground fish with the flour and a pinch of salt, and shape into small balls.
Fry in the oil over medium heat, turning occasionally, 5-6 minutes until golden and cooked through.
Simmer the sugar, soy sauce, and jalapeno with a splash of water into a thin, sweet-savory dipping sauce.
A beloved Filipino street food snack, sold on skewers.

name: Filipino Banana Cue
steps: Fry the whole banana in the oil, sprinkling with the brown sugar as it fries.
Cook 3-4 minutes per side, until the sugar caramelizes into a sticky coating.
Serve warm, traditionally on a skewer.
steps_detailed: Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat.
Fry the whole peeled banana, sprinkling generously with the brown sugar as it cooks, 4-6 minutes per side, until the sugar caramelizes into a deep, sticky, glossy coating.
Serve warm, traditionally threaded onto a bamboo skewer - a classic Filipino street food, especially popular after school.

name: Filipino Halo-Halo
steps: Sweeten the beans with a little of the sugar.
Layer the beans, jackfruit, and coconut milk in a glass.
Serve chilled, ideally over crushed ice.
steps_detailed: Sweeten the beans with a little of the sugar.
Layer the beans, jackfruit, sliced banana, and shredded coconut in a tall glass.
Pour the coconut milk over the top and serve chilled, ideally over crushed ice - halo-halo, meaning 'mix-mix,' is a beloved Filipino cold dessert combining many sweet ingredients.

name: Filipino Leche Flan
steps: Melt the sugar in a small dish until it turns amber, then swirl to coat the bottom.
Whisk the eggs, condensed milk, and milk together and pour over the caramel.
Steam or bake in a water bath at 325F for 35-40 minutes until just set.
steps_detailed: Melt the sugar in a small dish over low heat until it turns deep amber, then carefully swirl to coat the bottom and sides.
Whisk the eggs, condensed milk, milk, and vanilla together until smooth, being careful not to create too many bubbles.
Pour over the caramel.
Steam over simmering water, or bake in a water bath at 325F (165C), for 35-45 minutes until just set with a gentle wobble.
Chill before unmolding onto a plate, letting the caramel run down the sides - leche flan is a treasured Filipino dessert, especially for celebrations.

name: Filipino Bibingka
steps: Grind the rice into a fine flour and mix with the coconut milk, egg, and sugar into a smooth batter.
Pour into a small buttered dish and bake at 375F for 20-25 minutes until set and golden on top.
Cool slightly before slicing.
steps_detailed: Grind the rice into a fine flour and whisk with the coconut milk, egg, and sugar into a smooth, pourable batter.
Pour into a small buttered baking dish.
Bake at 375F (190C) for 22-28 minutes until set and golden on top.

Brush with the melted butter and scatter with shredded coconut while still warm - bibingka, traditionally baked in banana leaf over hot coals, is a beloved Filipino rice cake, especially popular during the Christmas season.

name: Filipino Vinegar Dipping Sauce (Sawsawan)
steps: Combine the vinegar, garlic, and jalapeno.
Season with salt and serve alongside fried or grilled foods.
steps_detailed: Combine the vinegar, garlic, jalapeno, soy sauce, and black pepper.
Let sit 10 minutes so the flavors meld.
Sawsawan, endlessly varied, is served alongside nearly every fried or grilled Filipino dish for dipping.

name: Filipino Banana Ketchup Style Glaze
steps: Blend the mashed banana with the tomato paste, vinegar, and sugar until smooth.
Simmer 5 minutes to thicken slightly.
Use as a glaze or dipping condiment.
steps_detailed: Blend the mashed banana with the tomato paste, vinegar, sugar, and black pepper until smooth.
Simmer over low heat 6-8 minutes until slightly thickened.
Banana ketchup is a beloved, uniquely Filipino condiment, invented during a WWII tomato shortage and never abandoned since.
